elements.sent-2411-001: Researchers and practitioners have long relied on human judges to provide information about the likelihood of future events.
elements.sent-2411-002: These judgments are especially useful for non-repeatable events about, e.g., geopolitics or popular culture, where there is little existing data that can be used to build predictive models of the phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-003: Even when data do exist to build predictive models, human judgments can be just as accurate \autocite{bejhyb2023, zelata2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-004: It is increasingly easy to obtain human forecasts remotely, so human judgments can be less costly than the expertise and technology required for predictive modeling, with little sacrifice in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-005: When recruiting human forecasters, we immediately take interest in individual differences among the forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-006: We want to focus our resources on forecasters who are exceptionally accurate and involved, which can allow us to select the best forecasters to rely on moving forward, or upweight their predictions in when aggregating groups of judgments \autocite{himtime2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: But assessment of forecasting accuracy takes time: it will often take months or years for forecasting questions have resolved, meaning it will often take months or years before we know which forecasters provide accurate forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-008: One solution to this dilemma involves proxy scoring \autocite[e.g.,][]{himwis2023,witata17 , whereby we use assumed outcomes of questions before those outcomes are truly known, often based on a crowd consensus.}
elements.sent-2411-009: We consider a second solution in this paper, which involves the use of cognitive tests to predict forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-010: Cognitive tests are advantageous because they can be quickly administered and scored.
elements.sent-2411-011: Past research has shown that the tests are correlated with forecasting accuracy \autocite{himata21, melpsyc15 , so that we can quickly obtain information about which forecasters will be accurate and which will be inaccurate.}
elements.sent-2411-012: But there are many potential cognitive tests that we could use, without much information about which tests we should use.
elements.sent-2411-013: Further, specific cognitive tests could serve specific purposes for predicting forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, certain cognitive tests can identify forecasters who may lack proficiency, while others distinguish top-tier forecasters, allowing for tailored assessments based on skill level.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this case, if we administer a large set of cognitive tests to all forecasters, then we are wasting resources because each test will only be relevant to assessing some forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-016: Further, we may want more information about good performers as compared to bad performers, so that good performers should take more cognitive tests than bad performers.
elements.sent-2411-017: We need ways to clarify which cognitive tests are good for which individuals, and to tailor administered tests to each individual's performance.
elements.sent-2411-018: This paper introduces adaptive cognitive testing procedures, using psychometric models to tailor cognitive tests to individual forecasters and to predict their forecasting skill levels.
elements.sent-2411-019: The ideas of selecting tests and tailoring tests to individual test-takers are related to adaptive testing procedures that have been developed in the context of item response modeling \autocite[e.g.,][]{draols99,magrai12,meiner99,vangla00,wai00 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Many readers will have experienced adaptive tests that measure readiness for university studies or for graduate school.
elements.sent-2411-021: We extend these traditional adaptive testing procedures to the cognitive testing domain, and we then apply the procedures to predict forecasting accuracy across two sets of forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-022: We show how the procedures can yield accurate forecaster assessments in reduced amounts of time, and we also obtain results about which cognitive tests are better or worse for predicting forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-023: In the pages below, we first give a conceptual overview of traditional adaptive testing methods.
elements.sent-2411-024: We then discuss how the methods can be extended to cognitive testing, and we describe an item response model that can handle scores that arise from cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-025: Next, we apply the model to two datasets, identifying which key cognitive tests most effectively predict forecasting skill, ultimately reducing testing time while maintaining accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-026: Next, we develop a fully adaptive testing procedure that tailors the sequence of cognitive tests to forecasters' performance, and we show that the procedure can reduce the time spent on cognitive testing.
elements.sent-2411-027: Finally, we discuss limitations and potential future developments.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this section, we give a brief conceptual overview of adaptive testing.
elements.sent-2411-029: We then deal with technical detail in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-030: The models that we consider are related to some that have been previously applied to forecasting by \textcite{merste16,merste17 and \textcite{bobud17 , though those authors did not consider adaptive tests.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The idea of adaptive testing is to choose an item to present to a person during a test, based on known item characteristics and on the person's previous responses during the test.
elements.sent-2411-032: If you have taken standardized tests like the GRE or GMAT, you may have experienced adaptive testing before.
elements.sent-2411-033: Adaptive testing is based on the notion that different items can provide different amounts of information about different individuals.
elements.sent-2411-034: For example, if a person has already answered many items incorrectly, then we do not learn much by giving that person a difficult item.
elements.sent-2411-035: We already know that the person will probably answer incorrectly.
elements.sent-2411-036: Similarly, if a person has already answered many items correctly, we do not learn much by giving that person an easy item.
elements.sent-2411-037: By adaptively presenting items that are uniquely suited to each individual, we can potentially obtain precise estimates of their proficiencies using shorter tests.
elements.sent-2411-038: Adaptive testing works by first estimating item properties during an ``item calibration'' step.
elements.sent-2411-039: Calibration involves determining each test item's difficulty and effectiveness across skill levels, which is used in later steps of the adaptive testing procedure.
elements.sent-2411-040: In this first step, no adaptive testing is used.
elements.sent-2411-041: We simply administer the items to a large group of representative people, fit item response models to their data, and then treat the resulting item parameter estimates as known.
elements.sent-2411-042: Once we have item parameter estimates, we can use those to compute item {\em information functions .}
elements.sent-2411-043: These functions summarize the amount of information that each item provides about participants of different proficiencies.
elements.sent-2411-044: They formalize the ideas presented in the previous section, where a difficult item provides more information about a person of high proficiency as compared to a person of low proficiency, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-045: Once we have the item information functions, they can be used to adaptively select items.
elements.sent-2411-046: That is, at each point of a test, we can compute an estimate of a person's proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-047: With that estimate, we can use item information functions to determine which of the remaining items should provide the most information about the person.
elements.sent-2411-048: Item information functions are related to the Fisher information matrix that is widely used to compute standard errors of maximum likelihood estimates.
elements.sent-2411-049: The intuition is that higher item information implies smaller standard errors, which implies that we gain more precise estimates of a person's proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-050: From a technical standpoint, item information functions involve second derivatives of an item response model's log-likelihood, where the derivatives are taken with respect to the person proficiency parameter (this parameter is often denoted $\theta$ in item response models).
elements.sent-2411-051: These derivatives have analytic forms for many item response models \autocite[e.g.,][]{wangra22 , which makes them suited for the adaptive testing purposes considered here.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The discussion so far has focused on individual items administered in a single test.
elements.sent-2411-053: But for many cognitive tests, only an overall score is recorded.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this case, each cognitive test is treated as an individual item, with information functions indicating which tests are best suited to high- and low-proficiency forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-055: We immediately encounter the problem of choosing a suitable item response model.
elements.sent-2411-056: For many cognitive tests, each person's score is an integer that could potentially be treated as continuous.
elements.sent-2411-057: The simplest thing to do would be to treat the scores as continuous and fit a normal model, where the model has item and person parameters similar to traditional IRT models.
elements.sent-2411-058: But this ignores the fact that most cognitive tests have a predefined lower and upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-059: To better characterize our bounded test scores, we employ a beta-distributed item response model \autocite{noedau07 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The beta distribution is particularly suited for bounded cognitive test scores, where a defined score range typically exists.
elements.sent-2411-061: The model's technical details remain similar to traditional item response models, except the model likelihood involves the beta distribution instead of the binomial distribution.
elements.sent-2411-062: We specifically use a variation of the \textcite{noedau07 model that was recently described by \textcite{molcur22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: For general information about modeling data via the beta distribution, see \textcite{smiver06 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Let $y_{ij}$ be the score of participant $i$ on cognitive test $j$, where the scores have been scaled to lie in $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let $z_{ij}$ be a 3-category ordinal variable indicating whether $y_{ij}$ is at a boundary or between the boundaries (1 if $y_{ij =0$; 2 if $y_{ij}$ is between 0 and 1; 3 if $y_{ij =1$).}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Our model has two parts: one part that predicts $z_{ij}$, and one part that predicts values of $y_{ij}$ between 0 and 1.
elements.sent-2411-067: As further described below, some parameters are shared between the two parts of the model.
elements.sent-2411-068: This part of the model can be written as \begin{equation}P(z_{ij} \leq c | \theta_i) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{jc} - \alpha_j \theta_i)\ \ c=1,2\end{equation} where $\gamma_{j1 < \gamma_{j2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We obtain probabilities of assuming individual categories by subtracting successive terms and making use of the fact that the three probabilities must sum to 1: \begin{equation}P(z_{ij} = 1 | \theta_i) = logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) P(z_{ij} = 2 | \theta_i) = logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) - logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) P(z_{ij} = 3 | \theta_i) = 1 - logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}P(z_{ij} = 1 | \theta_i) = logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) P(z_{ij} = 2 | \theta_i) = logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) - logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) P(z_{ij} = 3 | \theta_i) = 1 - logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: The $\alpha_j$ parameter is commonly called the discrimination parameter, while the $\gamma_j$ parameters help account for the base rates of boundary and non-boundary responses.
elements.sent-2411-071: When $z_{ij = 2$, the response $y_{ij}$ could lie anywhere between 0 and 1.}
elements.sent-2411-072: We use a beta item response model here, where the $\alpha_j$ and $\theta_i$ parameters are shared with the graded response submodel described above.
elements.sent-2411-073: The beta item response model assumes independence of test scores given $\theta_i$: \begin{equation}y_{ij} \mid \theta_i \sim \text{Beta}(a_{ij}, b_{ij})\end{equation} where the Beta distribution follows the traditional parameterization, with two positive shape parameters $a$ and $b$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Under this parameterization, our model assumes the following, unintuitive form: \begin{equation}a_{ij} = \exp \left ( \frac{\beta_j + \alpha_j \theta_i + \omega_j}{2} \right ) b_{ij} = \exp \left ( \frac{-(\beta_j + \alpha_j \theta_i) + \omega_j}{2} \right ).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: These expressions are relatively simple, and they make it clear that there are three parameters per test and one parameter per person.
elements.sent-2411-076: But it is not so easy to recognize this as an item response model.
elements.sent-2411-077: For the purposes of understanding the model, we write the mean of the beta distribution as $\mu_{ij = a_{ij /(a_{ij + b_{ij )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The expressions above lead us to a traditional, two-parameter item response function on $\mu_{ij}$ \autocite[see][]{noedau07 : \begin{equation}\mu_{ij} = \text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_j + \alpha_j \theta_i),\end{equation} where $\beta_j$ is often called the ``easiness'' parameter, and $\alpha_j$ is the discrimination parameter from the graded response submodel.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Further, $\text{logit ^{-1 (x) = (1 + \exp(-x))^{-1}$ is the inverse logit link function, used to account for the fact that $\mu_{ij}$ must lie in $(0,1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Because the beta distribution is bounded on both sides, the variance of the beta distribution is partly influenced by $\mu_{ij}$.
elements.sent-2411-081: To account for response heterogeneity across cognitive tests, our third test parameter $\omega_j$ helps predict the precision (inverse variance) of the beta distribution.
elements.sent-2411-082: With this parameter, the variance of the beta distribution is \begin{equation}\text{Var}(y_{ij} | \theta_i) = \frac{\mu_{ij} (1 - \mu_{ij})}{1 + 2\exp(\frac{\omega_j}{2})\cosh(\frac{\beta_j + \alpha_j \theta_i}{2})},\end{equation} so that the variance becomes larger when $\mu_{ij}$ is near .5 and when $\omega_j$ is negative.
elements.sent-2411-083: Combining the developments from the previous sections, we can write the overall model as \begin{equation}p(y_{ij} | \theta_i) = \begin{cases} \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) y_{ij} = 0 \left ( \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) - \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} + \alpha_j \theta_i) \right ) \times \text{Beta}(a_{ij}, b_{ij}) 0 < y_{ij} < 1 1 - \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) y_{ij} = 1. \end{cases} \label{eq:lik}\end{equation} The information function for each cognitive test, which can be used for adaptive testing purposes, is obtained by taking second derivatives of the resulting model likelihood with respect to $\theta_i$.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}p(y_{ij} | \theta_i) =  logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) y_{ij} = 0 \left ( logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) - logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} + \alpha_j \theta_i) \right ) \times Beta(a_{ij}, b_{ij}) 0 < y_{ij} < 1 1 - logit^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) y_{ij} = 1.  \label{eq:lik}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this section, we clarify and justify some specific modeling choices that we made.
elements.sent-2411-085: These choices involve the handling of boundary test scores, and the assumption that person proficiency is unidimensional.
elements.sent-2411-086: We address each choice separately below.
elements.sent-2411-087: First, our model follows the work of \textcite{molcur22 in that we explicitly model boundary responses.}
elements.sent-2411-088: In traditional models involving the beta distribution, exact values of 0 and 1 are impossible.
elements.sent-2411-089: So it is customary to change 0s to .0001 or some other small value, and to change 1s to .9999 or some other large value.
elements.sent-2411-090: The cognitive tests that we employ in this paper do not always have hard boundary values, so we instead use empirical boundaries, i.e., the smallest and largest observed scores on each test.
elements.sent-2411-091: The use of empirical boundaries is imprecise because there remains the possibility that future respondents receive scores that are more extreme than the empirical boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-092: Such extreme scores would be censored to the empirical boundary that was already set, which makes it more difficult to assess respondents who are exceptionally skilled or exceptionally unskilled.
elements.sent-2411-093: While our current treatment of boundaries works reasonably well in the applications, we further consider this issue in the General Discussion.
elements.sent-2411-094: Second, our model assumes that person proficiency is unidimensional.
elements.sent-2411-095: In other words, the model assumes that a single number fully describes each person's proficiency across all the tests.
elements.sent-2411-096: This assumption is surely incorrect, because there are many aspects of a person's proficiency that may lead them to do better or worse on specific tests.
elements.sent-2411-097: But we make this assumption for two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-098: First, our overall goal is to use the cognitive tests to gauge future forecasting accuracy, so it is beneficial to have a one-number summary of each person's performance.
elements.sent-2411-099: In other words, we are asking the model to reduce all the cognitive test scores into the best one-number summary possible.
elements.sent-2411-100: Second, additional dimensions of proficiency can lead to noisier estimates for each dimension, which may hamper our ability to predict future forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-101: We also return to this issue in the General Discussion.
elements.section-2411-003: Application 1: Test Information and Correlates with Forecasting
elements.sent-2411-102: We first fit the model to an initial dataset of 160 participants.
elements.sent-2411-103: This allows us to examine the model's general fit to the cognitive test scores, and it also allows us to obtain preliminary information functions for each cognitive test.
elements.sent-2411-104: The first round of data collection was an exploratory pilot study.
elements.sent-2411-105: It included a screening survey, followed by three weekly surveys.
elements.sent-2411-106: A total of 160 participants were recruited on Facebook and responded to the surveys.
elements.sent-2411-107: One of the three surveys (after screening) involved forecasting tasks, while the other two surveys involved the cognitive tests that are of interest here.
elements.sent-2411-108: The cognitive tests were all intended to be simple measures of cognitive reasoning and/or numeracy.
elements.sent-2411-109: Table~\ref{tab:ctests shows the tests that were used, along with key references for the tests.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The Adult Decision Making Competence test had three subscales, the Shipley test had two subscales, the Impossible Question test had three separate scores (one for the impossible questions and two for the ``possible,'' regular questions), and the Bayesian updating and denominator neglect tasks each had two versions.
elements.sent-2411-111: A more detailed description of the cognitive tasks can be found in the preregistration for a larger follow-up study (\url{https://osf.io/q2e94/ ), which is considered in a later section of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The forecasting questions, which we will examine later, were selected from a pool of questions from previous forecasting tournaments.
elements.sent-2411-113: The questions involved a range of topics including economics, politics, and popular entertainment, all of which could be understood by laypeople.
elements.sent-2411-114: All of the questions involved time series, so that participants could always predict the value of the time series at a future date, and so that the questions could be reused for different resolution dates.
elements.sent-2411-115: Further detail about the data collection can be found in the project's Open Science Foundation repository: \url{https://osf.io/q2e94/ .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Forecasts were elicited via quantile judgments, with forecast accuracy being measured via an {\em S-score \autocite{che22,grulic17,joswin09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-117: This is a strictly proper scoring rule for quantile forecasts that is based on the difference between each quantile estimate and the realized outcome, with larger penalties occurring when the outcome is extreme relative to the quantile estimates.
elements.sent-2411-118: Forecaster accuracy was summarized via an average S-score across questions.
elements.sent-2411-119: After fitting our model to the cognitive test scores, we examine the relationship between participants' performance on the cognitive tests and their average S-score.
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Cognitive tests and primary references.} \label{tab:ctests} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{ll} Test Name References Adult Decision Making Competence \textcite{bru07} Bayesian Updating \textcite{phi66} Berlin Numeracy \textcite{cokgal12} Cognitive Reflection \textcite{top13} Coherence Forecasting \textcite{hobud24} Denominator Neglect \textcite{kir92} \textcite{mik15} Graph Literacy \textcite{gal10} Impossible Question \textcite{ben22} Leapfrog \textcite{knox12} Matrix Reasoning \textcite{mat10} Number Series \textcite{die16} \textcite{himata21} Shipley General Intelligence \textcite{ship09} Time Series \textcite{debvan21} \textcite{reihar11} \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-120: Our sample size of 160 participants is small for item response applications, an issue that we consider more later.
elements.sent-2411-121: About 11\% of test scores were missing, and we did not do anything sophisticated to model missingness.
elements.sent-2411-122: The model estimation involves the traditional ``missing at random'' assumption, where we use all observed data and ignore missing data.
elements.sent-2411-123: We fit the model to the test scores via MCMC with Stan, saving 1,000 posterior samples from each of 3 chains after 1,000 warmup iterations.
elements.sent-2411-124: Model convergence was judged by the Rhat and effective sample size metrics \autocite[e.g.,][]{vehgel21 , neither of which indicated any estimation problems.}
elements.sent-2411-125: In our model, we used mildly informative prior distributions that constrain parameters to plausible values of the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-126: Our specific prior distributions were \begin{equation}\beta_j \sim N(0,2) \alpha_j \sim logN(0,1) \omega_j \sim N(0,10) \gamma_{j1} \sim N(-2,1) \gamma_{j2} \sim N(2,1),\end{equation} where normal distributions are parameterized via standard deviations and where $j=1,\ldots,20$.
elements.sent-2411-127: These priors are on the logit scale, which is similar to the z-score (or probit) scale.
elements.sent-2411-128: So we generally expect model predictions to be between $-4$ and $+4$ (on the logit scale), with increases of 1 reflecting relatively large increases in model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-129: This implies that the above priors are not as informative as they may appear at first glance.
elements.sent-2411-130: The appendix includes pairwise correlations between all 20 of the cognitive tests (treating the test scores as continuous), which is informative about the degree to which each individual test is related to the others.
elements.sent-2411-131: We focus here on our modeling results, which are divided into three subsections below.
elements.sent-2411-132: First, we examine overall fit of the model to the data.
elements.sent-2411-133: Next, we examine the information provided by each cognitive test, as estimated by our item response model.
elements.sent-2411-134: Finally, we use the estimated test information to select specific tests, and we examine how the selected tests are related to forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-135: Figure~\ref{fig:zrep is a posterior predictive check to ensure that the model is describing the data reasonably well.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In this figure, the black curves are smoothed histograms that represent the observed distribution of scores on each cognitive test.
elements.sent-2411-137: The blue curves represent the model's posterior predictive distribution of the scores.
elements.sent-2411-138: The blue curves collectively represent uncertainty in model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-139: This figure shows that the model accurately reproduces cognitive test scores, which supports the idea that the model can supply accurate, real-time forecaster assessments.

elements.sent-2411-140: The model provides an estimate of each person's proficiency across the tests.
elements.sent-2411-141: If the model is to be useful, we should expect the test proficiency estimates to be related to each person's eventual forecasting performance.
elements.sent-2411-142: We obtained each person's average S-score, which represents a one-number summary of forecasting performance.
elements.sent-2411-143: We then examined the relationship between S-scores and the model's proficiency estimates from the cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-144: This relationship is important because it provides evidence that cognitive test proficiency is predictive of forecasting proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-145: Results are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:fscores , where cognitive test proficiency is on the x-axis and forecasting S-scores are on the y-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-146: We see a negative relationship because smaller S-scores are better, and larger proficiency scores are better.
elements.sent-2411-147: The figure shows a correlation of $-0.69$, providing evidence that the model-based person estimates from the cognitive tests are strongly related to eventual forecasting performance.
elements.sent-2411-148: For comparison, we also computed an average standardized cognitive test score for each person, which does not involve any model estimates.
elements.sent-2411-149: The correlation between this average score and forecasting performance is also $-0.69$.
elements.sent-2411-150: This helps us build confidence that the model is providing a faithful representation of the data.
elements.sent-2411-151: More generally, these high correlations show that the cognitive test scores are predictive of eventual forecasting performance.
elements.sent-2411-152: Given that the model is behaving reasonably, we turn to the information functions of each cognitive test.
elements.sent-2411-153: Figure~\ref{fig:postmeaninf shows the posterior mean information functions, where person proficiency (on the cognitive tests) is on the x-axis and information is on the y-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Person proficiency is defined on the same scale as z-scores, where 0 represents a person at the 50th percentile and positive values represent people above the 50th percentile.
elements.sent-2411-155: The information values on the y-axis are bounded from below at 0.
elements.sent-2411-156: This figure shows that the cognitive tests generally provide more information about people of lower proficiency, as compared to people of higher proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-157: This is because the curves tend to be higher for proficiency values below 0.
elements.sent-2411-158: We see that Raven matrices, denominator neglect, and easy version of Bayesian updating are most informative, especially people whose proficiency is near the 50th percentile.
elements.sent-2411-159: Those metrics also tend to be relatively informative about people of higher proficiency, though the magnitude of information decreases near the right side of the figure.
elements.sent-2411-160: Figure~\ref{fig:postsampinf shows information separately for each scale, where we also include posterior uncertainty in the information curves.}
elements.sent-2411-161: This figure shows that the uncertainty for uninformative scales (low and flat curves) tends to be low, as compared to uncertainty for more informative scales (high and peaked curves).
elements.sent-2411-162: While there is definitely uncertainty in scale information, the figure shows that the uncertainty is not so large that we cannot distinguish between scales.
elements.sent-2411-163: For example, even after considering posterior uncertainty, we could still conclude that the Raven matrices are more informative than the Berlin numeracy test.
elements.sent-2411-164: A problem associated with Figure~\ref{fig:postsampinf is that it does not account for the amount of time that it takes to complete each test.}
elements.sent-2411-165: If the tests with the most information each take a long time to complete, then we might still be better off administering a series of quick tests.
elements.sent-2411-166: To address this issue, Figure~\ref{fig:infbytime shows the amount of information per minute that each cognitive test provides (which is based on the average amount of time that each test takes to complete).}
elements.sent-2411-167: The Bayesian update task and Raven matrices have now disappeared from the top, while Cognitive Reflection and Denominator Neglect take their place.
elements.sent-2411-168: The latter tasks took less time to administer.
elements.sent-2411-169: To select a subset of cognitive tests for administration, we can focus on the item information curves in Figure~\ref{fig:infbytime .}
elements.sent-2411-170: If we want to administer tests that are most informative about an average forecaster, we would find the curves that are highest for person proficiency of 0.
elements.sent-2411-171: These include cognitive reflection, denominator neglect, and Shipley vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-172: If we want to administer tests that are most informative about the top 10\% of forecasters, we find the curves that are highest for (say) person proficiency near 1.3 (this is the z-score that cuts off the top 10\% of the distribution).
elements.sent-2411-173: These are generally the same tests, with the possible addition of Berlin numeracy.
elements.sent-2411-174: There are also some tests that provide little information across the full range of proficiency (Leapfrog, Impossible Question, General Knowledge calibration), suggesting that they could be safely removed.
elements.sent-2411-175: To begin to explore the utility of these ideas, we computed each participant's average standardized score on a subset of cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-176: Then we examined the relationship between these scores and their eventual forecasting S-scores.

elements.sent-2411-231: We now combine results from the previous sections to develop an adaptive testing procedure, and to apply the adaptive testing procedure to the Study 1 data.
elements.sent-2411-232: We examine how the procedure tailors the sequence of cognitive tests to each participant, and the time that it takes to achieve reliable estimates of participant performance.
elements.sent-2411-233: The adaptive testing procedure that we implemented includes the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-234: Start all participants on the test that is most informative for the average participant.
elements.sent-2411-235: Score each participant using all tests that they have completed so far.
elements.sent-2411-236: Based on each participant's running score, administer the test that is expected to be most informative and that has not yet been administered to the participant.
elements.sent-2411-237: Repeat Steps 2 and 3 until all tests have been administered.
elements.sent-2411-238: In Step 2 above, we use the estimated item parameters from Study 2 to score each participant (obtaining empirical Bayes estimates of their $\theta_i$ parameters).
elements.sent-2411-239: In Step 3, we use two types of information from Study 2: the usual test information (shown in Figure~\ref{fig:infcomp ), and test information per minute (similar to Figure~\ref{fig:infbytime ).}}
elements.sent-2411-240: The latter may be useful because it attempts to gain the most information about each participant in the shortest amount of time.
elements.sent-2411-241: We apply the adaptive testing procedure to all 97 Study 1 participants with complete data.
elements.sent-2411-242: We use 18 of the 20 test scores; we excluded the Impossible Question and General Knowledge Calibration tests because (i) they were generally uninformative and (ii) they come from the same task as the General Knowledge Percent Correct score, which would double-count the timing calculations that we present.
elements.sent-2411-243: At the start of the adaptive testing procedure, we pretend that the participants have not completed any cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-244: We then sequentially reveal participants' scores to the adaptive testing procedure, based on the tests that the procedure selects.
elements.sent-2411-245: In this way, we can examine how the procedure would have behaved, had we actually used it during the Study 1 data collection.

elements.sent-2411-272: In this paper, we applied item response models and adaptive testing principles to yield insights into the relationship between cognitive tests and forecasting proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-273: We applied a beta item response model to cognitive test scores and estimated the test information curves, which helped us discern the suitability of each test to participants of varying proficiencies.
elements.sent-2411-274: We used the information curves to select informative tests and discard uninformative tests, reducing the time taken to administer the tests with little sacrifice to predictive accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-275: We verified that these test selections remained worthwhile via a second, independent dataset, and we then showed how the information curves could be used to develop an adaptive testing procedure that works in real time.
elements.sent-2411-276: In this section, we consider limitations and future developments.
elements.sent-2411-277: While our model performance was reasonable, there are a few places where it could be improved and where our conclusions should be qualified.
elements.sent-2411-278: We expand on these points below.
elements.sent-2411-279: First, the beta IRT model required us to set bounds on each cognitive test's possible scores, and the exact bounds are not always well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-280: For example, the Leapfrog task has theoretical boundaries of 0 and 400, but it is nearly impossible to achieve a score near either bound.
elements.sent-2411-281: We therefore used empirical boundaries based on the Study 1 data.
elements.sent-2411-282: This use of empirical boundaries limits the possible scores that could be achieved in future scoring.
elements.sent-2411-283: For example, if a Study 2 participant scored higher than the empirical Study 1 upper bound, then that participant would be treated as though their score equaled the empirical Study 1 upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-284: This potentially limits our ability to distinguish between exceptional participants (exceptionally proficient or unproficient).
elements.sent-2411-285: An alternative would be to scrap the boundaries, employing a Gaussian IRT model in place of a beta IRT model.
elements.sent-2411-286: The information functions under a Gaussian model are flat, though, which prevents us from tailoring tests to different participants.
elements.sent-2411-287: Related to the boundary issue, some of the cognitive test scores are defined as the number of items correct.
elements.sent-2411-288: For these tests, it may be better to use a binomial IRT model (setting the binomial $n$ parameter to the total number of items) instead of a beta IRT model.
elements.sent-2411-289: Use of the binomial would remove the need to set bounds for some cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-290: The information functions would then be of a different form, depending on whether the cognitive test uses a beta or a binomial likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-291: These different information functions may further heighten our ability to predict forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-292: Finally, as we mentioned near the start, our beta IRT model assumed a single dimension of proficiency across all the cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-293: This assumption helps limit the complexity of the model, which potentially improves model predictions (including test information) for new datasets \autocite[e.g.,][]{mersaw20 .}
elements.sent-2411-294: But this assumption is sure to be incorrect, which qualifies our conclusions about the test information functions.
elements.sent-2411-295: There remains the possibility that some of the low-information tests from our study are informative about certain aspects of forecasting, but we cannot see that when we lump together all the cognitive tests in one dimension.
elements.sent-2411-296: It would be possible to fit beta IRT models with multiple dimensions of proficiency, with each test having a unique information function for each dimension.
elements.sent-2411-297: In designing an adaptive testing procedure, we then must decide which dimension(s) of proficiency to prioritize.
elements.sent-2411-298: There quickly become many possibilities here, both in number of proficiency dimensions and in cognitive test selection.
elements.sent-2411-299: The simplicity of a unidimensional model is advantageous here, in that it reduces the forking paths of modeling attributes and adaptive test designs.
elements.sent-2411-300: Along with multidimensional IRT models, future work may involve applying these models to related datasets.
elements.sent-2411-301: First, while each participant currently receives a single score on each cognitive test, we can consider applying an IRT model to the individual items within each cognitive test.
elements.sent-2411-302: If there are a few highly-informative items in each test, such an IRT model would help us isolate those items, leading to an adaptive test administration that takes even less time than those examined here.
elements.sent-2411-303: Second, we may apply the adaptive testing approach to ``repeatable'' forecasting questions, such as questions that require forecasters to predict the value of a specific stock one month in advance.
elements.sent-2411-304: We could estimate the information of each forecasting question, then present different questions to forecasters based on their forecasting performance to date.
elements.sent-2411-305: Combining this idea with multidimensional IRT models, we may select questions for each forecaster depending on the specific dimensions where each forecaster excels.
elements.sent-2411-306: We anticipate that large datasets will be necessary to estimate the parameters of such a model with enough precision to be useful.
elements.sent-2411-307: The models and procedures used in this paper appear fruitful for continued application to cognitive tests, to forecasting questions, and to combinations thereof.
elements.sent-2411-308: They help us find the best tests for assessing participants, and they also allow us to develop shorter administrations that are as good as longer administrations.
elements.sent-2411-309: The procedures are also efficient enough to be used in real time, after a model has been fit to a base dataset.
elements.sent-2411-310: We look forward to further developments along these lines in the future.

elements.sent-2411-314: Researchers and practitioners have long relied on human forecasters to provide information about the likelihood of future events.
elements.sent-2411-315: These forecasts are especially useful for non-repeatable events in, say, geopolitics or popular culture, where there is little existing data that can be used to build predictive models of such events \cite<e.g.,>{atares17,walbud83 .}
elements.sent-2411-316: Alternatively, when data do exist to build predictive models, human forecasts can be just as accurate as the models \cite{bejhyb2023, zelata2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-317: In many cases, human forecasts can be less costly than the expertise and technology required for predictive modeling, often with little sacrifice in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-318: When recruiting human forecasters, we immediately take interest in individual differences between the forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-319: Previous research \cite{melsto15,melung14 has demonstrated the existence of highly skilled forecasters who provide accurate forecasts across domains, which \citeA{tet16 termed {\em superforecasters .}}}
elements.sent-2411-320: We want to focus our resources on these exceptionally accurate forecasters, so that we can obtain accurate and timely aggregate forecasts \cite<e.g.,>{himtime2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-321: But it often takes months or years for forecasting questions to resolve, which makes it difficult to assess individual differences in forecaster accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-322: One solution to this dilemma involves comparison of individual forecasters to their peers \cite<e.g.,>{budche15,chebud16,himwis2023,witata17 , prior to knowing the outcomes of the forecasting questions.}
elements.sent-2411-323: Cognitive tests are advantageous for our purposes because they can be quickly scored.
elements.sent-2411-324: Past research has shown that the tests are correlated with forecasting accuracy \cite{atahim22,himata21, melpsyc15 , making it possible to quickly obtain information about which forecasters are likely to be accurate.}
elements.sent-2411-325: But there are many potential cognitive tests that one {\em could use, without much information about which tests one {\em should use.}}
elements.sent-2411-326: Further, specific cognitive tests may serve specific purposes for predicting forecasting accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-327: For example, certain cognitive tests may help identify forecasters who lack any proficiency, while others may help distinguish top-tier forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-328: If we administer a large set of cognitive tests to all forecasters, then we waste resources when each test is only relevant for some of the forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-329: Further, if we want information about good forecasters as opposed to bad forecasters, then the good forecasters should complete more cognitive tests than the bad forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-330: The idea of selecting tests and tailoring them to individual forecasters is related to adaptive testing procedures that have been developed in the context of item response modeling \cite<e.g.,>{draols99,magrai12,meiner99,vangla00,wai00 .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Many readers will have experienced adaptive tests that measure readiness for university studies.
elements.sent-2411-332: We extend these traditional adaptive testing procedures to the cognitive testing domain, and we then apply the procedures to predict forecasting accuracy across two independent sets of forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-333: We show how our adaptive cognitive tests can yield accurate assessments of forecasters' skill levels in reduced time, and we also obtain results about which cognitive tests are generally better or worse for predicting forecasting accuracy across all skill levels.
elements.sent-2411-334: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-335: We first give a conceptual overview of traditional adaptive testing methods.
elements.sent-2411-336: We then show how the methods can be extended to cognitive testing.
elements.sent-2411-337: Next, we apply the model to two independent datasets, identifying cognitive tests that effectively predict different levels of forecasting skill.
elements.sent-2411-338: We then discuss the technical details in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-339: The models that we consider are related to some that have been previously applied to forecasting by \citeA{merste16,merste17 and \citeA{bobud17 , though those authors did not consider adaptive tests.}}
elements.sent-2411-340: The idea of adaptive testing is to choose a question to present to a person during a test, based on known question characteristics and based on the person's previous responses during the test.
elements.sent-2411-341: Standardized tests like the GRE or GMAT are examples of adaptive testing.
elements.sent-2411-342: Adaptive testing is based on the notion that different questions can provide different amounts of information about different individuals.
elements.sent-2411-343: For example, if a person has already answered many questions incorrectly, then little is gained from presenting that person with a difficult question.
elements.sent-2411-344: Similarly, if a person has already answered many questions correctly, we do not learn much by presenting that person with an easy question.
elements.sent-2411-345: By adaptively presenting questions that are uniquely suited to each individual, we can potentially obtain a precise estimate of their proficiency using shorter tests.
elements.sent-2411-346: Adaptive testing works by first estimating question properties during an ``item calibration'' step.
elements.sent-2411-347: Calibration involves determining properties of each test question (e.g., the question's difficulty), with these properties being used in later steps of the adaptive testing procedure.
elements.sent-2411-348: We simply present the questions to a large sample of representative people, fit item response models to their data, and treat the resulting parameter estimates as known.
elements.sent-2411-349: Once we have the parameter estimates, we can use those to compute question {\em information functions .}
elements.sent-2411-350: These functions summarize the amount of information that each question provides about participants of different proficiencies.
elements.sent-2411-351: They formalize the ideas presented in the previous paragraph, where a difficult question provides more information about a person of high proficiency as compared to a person of low proficiency, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-352: Once we have the question information functions, they can be used to adaptively select questions.
elements.sent-2411-353: That is, at each point in a test, we can compute an estimate of a person's proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-354: With that estimate, we can use question information functions to determine which of the remaining questions will provide the most information about the person.
elements.sent-2411-355: Question information functions are related to the Fisher information matrix that is widely used to compute standard errors of model parameters estimated via maximum likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-356: The intuition is that a high amount of question information implies a smaller standard error, which in turn implies a more precise estimate of a person's proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-357: From a technical standpoint, question information functions involve second derivatives of an item response model's log-likelihood, where the derivatives are taken with respect to each person's proficiency parameter (a parameter that is often denoted $\theta$ in item response models).
elements.sent-2411-358: These derivatives have analytic forms for many item response models \cite<e.g.,>{wangra22 , which makes them suited for the adaptive testing purposes considered here.}
elements.sent-2411-359: The discussion so far has focused on individual questions administered in a single test.
elements.sent-2411-360: These individual questions are often scored as ``correct'' or ``incorrect.'' By contrast, for many cognitive tests, only an overall score is recorded.
elements.sent-2411-361: In this case, we can treat each cognitive test as an individual question, with information functions indicating which tests are best suited to high- and low-proficiency forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-362: The simplest thing to do would be to treat the scores as continuous and fit a Gaussian model, where the model has question and person parameters similar to traditional item response models.
elements.sent-2411-363: To better characterize our bounded test scores, we employ a beta-distributed item response model \cite{noedau07 .}
elements.sent-2411-364: We specifically use a variation of the \citeA{noedau07 model that was recently described by \citeA{molcur22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-365: For general information about modeling data via the beta distribution, see \citeA{smiver06 .}
elements.sent-2411-366: Let $z_{ij}$ be a three-category ordinal variable indicating whether $y_{ij}$ is at a boundary or between the boundaries (1 if $y_{ij =0$; 2 if $y_{ij}$ is between 0 and 1; 3 if $y_{ij =1$).}}
elements.sent-2411-367: This part of the model can be written as \begin{equation}P(z_{ij} \leq c | \theta_i) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{jc} - \alpha_j \theta_i)\ \ c=1,2,\end{equation} where $\gamma_{j1 < \gamma_{j2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-368: Further, $\text{logit ^{-1 (x) = (1 + \exp(-x))^{-1}$ is the inverse logit link function, which converts an unbounded prediction to a prediction that lies in $(0,1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: The beta item response model assumes independence of test scores given $\theta_i$: \begin{equation}y_{ij} \mid \theta_i \sim \text{Beta}(a_{ij}, b_{ij}),\end{equation} where the beta distribution follows the traditional parameterization, with two positive shape parameters $a$ and $b$.
elements.sent-2411-370: These expressions are relatively simple, and they make it clear that there are three parameters per test (those with a $j$ subscript) and one parameter per person ($i$ subscript).
elements.sent-2411-371: The expressions above then lead us to a traditional, two-parameter item response function on $\mu_{ij}$ \cite<see>{noedau07 : \begin{equation}\mu_{ij} = \text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_j + \alpha_j \theta_i),\end{equation} where $\beta_j$ is often called the ``easiness'' parameter, and $\alpha_j$ is the discrimination parameter from the graded response submodel.}
elements.sent-2411-372: With this parameter, the variance of the beta distribution can be written as \begin{equation}\text{Var}(y_{ij} | \theta_i) = \frac{\mu_{ij} (1 - \mu_{ij})}{1 + 2\exp(\frac{\omega_j}{2})\cosh(\frac{\beta_j + \alpha_j \theta_i}{2})},\end{equation} where the variance becomes larger when $\mu_{ij}$ is near 0.5 and when $\omega_j$ is negative.
elements.sent-2411-373: Combining the results from the previous sections, we can write the overall model as \begin{equation}\label{eq:lik} p(y_{ij} | \theta_i) = \begin{cases} \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} - \alpha_j \theta_i) y_{ij} = 0 \left ( \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) - \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j1} + \alpha_j \theta_i) \right ) \times \text{Beta}(a_{ij}, b_{ij}) 0 < y_{ij} < 1 1 - \text{logit}^{-1}(\gamma_{j2} - \alpha_j \theta_i) y_{ij} = 1. \end{cases}\end{equation} The information function for each cognitive test, which can be used for adaptive testing purposes, is obtained by taking second derivatives of the resulting model likelihood with respect to $\theta_i$.

elements.sent-2411-374: In this subsection, we clarify and justify some specific modeling choices that we made.
elements.sent-2411-375: These choices involve the handling of boundary test scores, and the assumption that proficiency is unidimensional.
elements.sent-2411-376: First, our model follows the work of \citeA{molcur22 in that we explicitly model boundary responses.}
elements.sent-2411-377: In traditional models involving the beta distribution, exact values of 0 and 1 are impossible, and it is customary to change 0s to 0.0001 or some other small value, and to change 1s to 0.9999 or some other large value.
elements.sent-2411-378: The cognitive tests that we employ in this paper do not always have hard boundary values, and we instead use empirical boundaries, i.e., the smallest and largest observed scores on each test.
elements.sent-2411-379: Such extreme scores would be censored by the empirical boundaries that were already set, which makes it more difficult to assess respondents who are exceptionally skilled or exceptionally unskilled.
elements.sent-2411-380: Second, our model assumes that each person's proficiency is unidimensional.
elements.sent-2411-381: In other words, the model assumes that a single number fully describes each person's proficiency across all the cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-382: First, our overall goal is to use the cognitive tests to gauge future forecasting accuracy, so that it is beneficial to have a one-number summary of each person's proficiency.
elements.sent-2411-383: In other words, we have the model reduce all the cognitive test scores into the best one-number summary possible.
elements.section-2411-010: Study 1: Test Information and Correlates with Forecasting
elements.sent-2411-384: The datasets that we use to develop adaptive tests were collected as part of a large project on forecasting proficiency assessment \cite{himzhu24 .}
elements.sent-2411-385: Our first study involves data from an exploratory pilot experiment where 170 participants completed a variety of cognitive tests and forecasting tasks, details of which appear below.
elements.sent-2411-386: Our second study, discussed later, involved over 1,000 participants and was similar to the pilot.
elements.sent-2411-387: The two studies allow us to compare the stability of model estimates over time and to examine out-of-sample model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-388: But for our first study, we examine the model's fit to the cognitive test scores, as well as the information functions for each cognitive test.
elements.sent-2411-389: Study 1 included a screening survey, followed by three weekly surveys, with a total of 170 participants being recruited on Facebook and responded to the surveys.
elements.sent-2411-390: Table~\ref{tab:ctests shows the tests that were used, along with key references for the tests, with further information about the rationale for these tests being found in \citeA{himzhu24 as well as the OSF repository at \url{https://osf.io/q2e94/ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-391: The Adult Decision Making Competence test has three subscales, the Shipley test has two subscales, the Impossible Question test has three separate scores (one for the impossible questions and two for the ``possible,'' regular questions), and the Bayesian Updating and Denominator Neglect tasks each have two versions.
elements.sent-2411-392: The forecasting questions were selected from a database of questions used in previous forecasting tournaments \cite{zouxia22 , with each participant forecasting 15 questions.}
elements.sent-2411-393: The specific questions used can be found in the project materials.
elements.sent-2411-394: Forecasts were elicited via quantile judgments, with forecast accuracy being measured via an {\em S-score \cite{che22,grulic17,joswin09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-395: This is a strictly proper scoring rule for quantile forecasts that is based on the absolute distance between each quantile estimate and the realized outcome, with larger penalties being imposed when the outcome is extreme relative to the quantile estimates.
elements.sent-2411-396: After fitting our model to the cognitive test scores, we examined the relationship between participants' performance on the cognitive tests and their average S-score.

elements.sent-2411-399: We did not do anything sophisticated to model missingness; model estimation involves the traditional ``missing at random'' assumption, where we use all observed data and ignore missing data.
elements.sent-2411-400: We fit the model to the test scores via Markov chain Monte Carlo with Stan, saving 1,000 posterior samples from each of 3 chains after 1,000 warmup iterations.
elements.sent-2411-401: Model convergence was judged by the Rhat and effective sample size metrics \cite<e.g.,>{vehgel21 , neither of which indicated estimation problems.}

elements.section-2411-011: Study 2: Model Stability and Out-of-Sample Predictions
elements.sent-2411-442: Study 2 was a larger experiment that was collected about eight months after Study 1.
elements.sent-2411-443: This second study allows us to examine the stability of the model estimates across datasets, and it also allows us to score new participants using the test parameter estimates from Study 1.
elements.sent-2411-444: The analyses provide further evidence of the model's utility for the purposes of adaptive testing, where cognitive tests are automatically selected based on a participant's performance on previous tests.
elements.sent-2411-445: The data collection was similar to that of Study 1, with the involvement of more participants and more forecasting questions.
elements.sent-2411-446: The 1194 participants in this study forecasted 36 time series questions, many of which overlapped with those used in Study 1 (see the Supplemental Material; some question stems were the same in Study 1 and Study 2, but the time frames differed).
elements.sent-2411-447: Participants completed the same cognitive tasks that were used in Study 1, and our model priors and estimation details remained the same as Study 1.
elements.sent-2411-448: Conventional Bayesian diagnostics again indicated that the model converged.
elements.sent-2411-449: As was done in Study 1, we can fit the item response model to our new dataset and use the estimated $\theta_i$ parameters as a measure of each participant's overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-450: Alternatively, we can use the existing question parameter estimates from Study 1 to score participants from Study 2.
elements.sent-2411-451: This scoring option is advantageous because it does not require us to fit a new model to the Study 2 data, so forecasters can be scored in real time as they complete cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-452: To do the scoring, we take the log-likelihood function implied by Equation~\eqref{eq:lik and fix the question parameters to their posterior means from Study 1.}
elements.sent-2411-453: We then numerically optimize the log-likelihood with respect to $\theta_i$, separately for each $i$ (where $i$ indexes Study 2 participants).
elements.sent-2411-454: This unidimensional optimization is much faster than fitting a full model to the new dataset.
elements.sent-2411-455: We begin by comparing the question parameter estimates across the two studies.
elements.sent-2411-456: Figure~\ref{fig:parcomp provides this comparison, where each panel is a question parameter, Study 1 estimates appear on the x-axis, and Study 2 estimates appear on the y-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-457: Each point represents one of the 20 cognitive tests that were administered in both Study 1 and Study 2, with the horizontal error bars being larger because there were fewer participants in Study 1 than in Study 2.
elements.sent-2411-458: The same is true of the omega parameters, which represent residual variability in the beta distribution.
elements.sent-2411-459: Because boundary responses are relatively rare, it is reasonable to assume that the larger sample sizes from Study 2 led to these differences.
elements.sent-2411-460: Specifically, larger sample size reduced the amount of shrinkage in the gamma parameters, leading the model to produce estimates that were more extreme.

elements.sent-2411-501: We used the test information curves to select informative tests and discard uninformative tests, reducing the time required to administer the tests without compromising predictive accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-502: We verified that the selected tests generalized to a second, independent dataset, and we then showed how the test information curves could be used to develop an adaptive testing procedure that works in real time.
elements.sent-2411-503: While our model performance was reasonable, there are places where it could be improved and where our conclusions should be qualified.
elements.sent-2411-504: First, the beta item response model required us to set bounds on each cognitive test's possible scores, and the exact bounds are not always well defined.
elements.sent-2411-505: This use of empirical boundaries limits the possible scores that could be achieved in future testing.
elements.sent-2411-506: This potentially limits our ability to distinguish between exceptional participants (exceptionally proficient or improficient).
elements.sent-2411-507: An alternative would be to scrap the boundaries by employing a Gaussian IRT model in place of the beta item response model.
elements.sent-2411-508: The information functions under the Gaussian model are flat, though, which prevents us from tailoring tests to different participants.
elements.sent-2411-509: Related to the boundary issue, some of the cognitive tests are scored in terms of the number of questions answered correctly.
elements.sent-2411-510: For these tests, it may be better to use a binomial item response model (setting the binomial $n$ parameter to the total number of questions) instead of a beta item response model.
elements.sent-2411-511: Use of the binomial model would remove the need to set bounds for some cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-512: Finally, as we mentioned in the Introduction, our beta item response model assumed a single dimension of proficiency across all the cognitive tests.
elements.sent-2411-513: This assumption helps limit the complexity of the model, which potentially improves model predictions (including test information) for new datasets \cite<e.g.,>{mersaw20 .}
elements.sent-2411-514: But this assumption is sure to be incorrect, which qualifies our conclusions about the test information functions: there remains the possibility that some of the low-information tests from our study are informative about certain aspects of forecasting, but we cannot see that when we lump together all the cognitive tests in one dimension.
elements.sent-2411-515: It would be possible to fit beta item response models with multiple dimensions of proficiency, with each test having a unique information function for each dimension.
elements.sent-2411-516: There are many possibilities here, both in terms of the number of proficiency dimensions and in terms of cognitive test selection.
elements.sent-2411-517: The simplicity of a unidimensional model is advantageous, in that it reduces the forking paths of modeling attributes and adaptive test designs.
elements.sent-2411-518: Along with multidimensional item response models, future work could explore applying these models to related datasets.
elements.sent-2411-519: First, while each participant currently receives a single score on each cognitive test, we could consider applying an item response model to the individual questions in each cognitive test.
elements.sent-2411-520: If there are a few highly informative questions in each test, such an item response model would help us isolate those questions, leading to an adaptive test that takes even less time than those examined here.
elements.sent-2411-521: Second, we may apply the adaptive testing approach to ``repeatable'' forecasting questions, such as Time Series questions with differing time horizons.
elements.sent-2411-522: We could estimate the information of each forecasting question, and then present different questions to forecasters based on their forecasting performance to date.
elements.sent-2411-523: Combining this idea with multidimensional item response models, we may select questions for each forecaster depending on the specific dimensions along which each forecaster excels.
elements.sent-2411-524: They help us find the best tests for assessing forecasters' proficiency, and they allow us to develop shorter adaptive tests that are as good as longer tests.
